questions.question_text: In South Dakota, a single agent represents:
questions.correct_answer: Only one party with full fiduciary duties
distractors: Both parties with limited disclosure
Neither party as a neutral facilitator
One party with reduced obligations
questions.explanation: A single agent (seller's or buyer's agent) represents only one party and owes full fiduciary duties including loyalty, utmost good faith, and fidelity. This is the most traditional form of agency in South Dakota real estate.

questions.question_text: A limited agent in South Dakota represents both buyer and seller. What consent is required?
questions.correct_answer: Informed written consent from both parties
distractors: Verbal consent from the buyer only
Written consent from the seller only
No special consent beyond standard disclosure
questions.explanation: Limited agency (dual representation) requires informed written consent from BOTH buyer and seller. The limited agent must treat both parties equally with no partiality. This is South Dakota's version of dual agency.

questions.question_text: Which duty does a South Dakota licensee owe to ALL clients regardless of agency type?
questions.correct_answer: Present all written offers in a timely manner
distractors: Negotiate the lowest possible price for the buyer
Share the other party's financial information
Recommend a specific lender or inspector
questions.explanation: Under SDCL 36-21A-132 through 136, all agents must present all written offers in a timely manner, regardless of existing contracts. Other universal duties include exercising reasonable skill and care, disclosing adverse material facts, and accounting for all money received.

questions.question_text: A licensee discovers a material defect in a listed property. Under SD agency law, what must they do?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the adverse material fact to all parties
distractors: Disclose only if the buyer specifically asks
Keep it confidential as the seller's agent
Report it to the SDREC before disclosure
questions.explanation: South Dakota law requires licensees to disclose adverse material facts known to them — this duty applies regardless of which party they represent. Concealing known defects violates the licensee's statutory obligations under SDCL 36-21A.

questions.question_text: Which obligation is specifically listed among a SD agent's duties to clients?
questions.correct_answer: Advise clients to seek expert advice when needed
distractors: Guarantee the property will appreciate in value
Provide legal opinions on contract disputes
Determine the exact market value of property
questions.explanation: SD agents must advise clients to seek expert advice on material matters beyond the licensee's expertise — such as legal, tax, structural, or environmental issues. Agents should not attempt to provide professional opinions outside their area of competence.

questions.question_text: When must a South Dakota licensee provide the Real Estate Relationships Disclosure form?
distractors: At the time of contract signing
Within 48 hours of the first showing
Only when requested by the client
questions.explanation: The disclosure must be provided at first substantive contact, defined as when the licensee begins discussing the buyer's financial qualifications, motives, or objectives that could harm their bargaining position if disclosed.

questions.question_text: A licensee begins discussing a buyer's pre-approval amount and ideal price range. This conversation qualifies as:
distractors: A casual conversation with no obligations
A protected negotiation under attorney privilege
A marketing discussion requiring no disclosure
questions.explanation: Substantive contact occurs when discussing a buyer's financial qualifications, motives, or objectives that could harm their bargaining position if disclosed. Talking about pre-approval amounts and price range clearly meets this definition.

questions.question_text: The Real Estate Relationships Disclosure form in South Dakota is:
questions.correct_answer: Neither a contract nor an addendum
distractors: A binding agency contract with the licensee
An addendum to the purchase agreement
A mandatory listing agreement supplement
questions.explanation: The SD disclosure form is informational — it explains the types of agency relationships available. It is neither a contract nor an addendum. A separate written agency agreement must still be executed to establish the actual relationship.

questions.question_text: In South Dakota, can a licensee begin working with a buyer before signing a written agency agreement?
questions.correct_answer: No, all relationships require written agreements
distractors: Yes, verbal agreements are sufficient initially
Yes, if the buyer signs within 72 hours
Yes, for the first three property showings
questions.explanation: South Dakota requires written agency agreements for ALL licensee relationships. No matter what type of relationship is formed (single agent, limited agent, appointed agent, or transaction broker), a written agreement must be entered into.

questions.question_text: A licensee asks a potential buyer about their motivation for selling their current home. Has substantive contact occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, discussing motives triggers the disclosure
distractors: No, only financial questions trigger disclosure
No, substantive contact only involves offers
Yes, but only if the conversation was recorded
questions.explanation: Substantive contact is defined as discussing the buyer's financial qualifications, motives, OR objectives that could harm their bargaining position. Asking about motivation for selling falls squarely within this definition.

questions.question_text: In South Dakota, an appointed agent provides what level of representation?
questions.correct_answer: Full representation to their assigned client
distractors: Limited representation to both parties equally
No representation as a neutral facilitator
Partial representation with reduced duties
questions.explanation: An appointed agent is selected by the responsible broker to provide FULL representation to a specific client, even when another licensee in the same firm represents the other party. This is an alternative to limited (dual) agency.

questions.question_text: A transaction broker in South Dakota may NOT do which of the following?
questions.correct_answer: Advise to the detriment of any party
distractors: Assist with completing transaction forms
Provide market data and property information
Help coordinate inspections and appraisals
questions.explanation: A transaction broker assists parties WITHOUT being an agent or advocate. The key restriction is that they may not advise to the detriment of any party. Parties are customers, not clients, meaning there is no fiduciary duty of loyalty.

questions.question_text: Two licensees in the same brokerage each represent opposing sides of a transaction. This arrangement is BEST described as:
distractors: Single agency for both parties
Transaction brokerage by default
An illegal conflict of interest
questions.explanation: When two licensees from the same firm represent opposite sides, the responsible broker can appoint each as the client's agent — providing full representation to each party. This is appointed agency, an alternative to limited (dual) agency.

questions.question_text: Under the 2024 NAR settlement rules, what must buyers in South Dakota sign before beginning home tours?
questions.correct_answer: A written buyer-broker agreement specifying the fee
distractors: A disclosure that the seller pays commission
A waiver of their right to representation
An acknowledgment of the listing agent's duties
questions.explanation: Effective August 2024, buyers must sign a written buyer-broker agreement specifying the agent's fee before touring homes. Commission amounts remain fully negotiable, and sellers may still choose to contribute toward the buyer's agent compensation.

questions.question_text: Parties working with a transaction broker in South Dakota are classified as:
distractors: Clients with full fiduciary protection
Principals under the agency agreement
Third parties with no relationship
questions.explanation: A transaction broker's parties are customers, not clients. This distinction is important — customers do not receive fiduciary duties like loyalty and full advocacy. The transaction broker assists with the transaction but does not represent either party.
